cards.back: A defined way for one program to request services/data from another.
HTTPS is HTTP encrypted with TLS — data in transit can't be read/tampered.
RAM is fast temporary memory (cleared on power-off); storage is permanent.
Someone else's computers in a data center, rented over the internet.
Recently used data kept close by so it loads faster next time.
Frontend is what you see/click; backend is server, logic, and database.
A precise step-by-step recipe to solve a problem.
Source code is public to read, use, and modify under a license.
A bit is one 0/1; a byte is 8 bits.
Organized storage to save, search, and update lots of data reliably.
A device's numeric address on a network, so data knows where to go.
Translates a domain name (example.com) into its IP address — the internet's phonebook.
A computer that "serves" responses to requests from other machines (clients).
A compiler translates code to machine code ahead of time; an interpreter runs it line by line.
A named box that stores a value your program can read and change.
Code that repeats a block until a condition is met — instead of copy-pasting.
A simple text format for structured data — keys and values — used everywhere in APIs.
It tracks changes to code over time so you can review, revert, and collaborate.
Scrambling data so only someone with the key can read it.
Software (Windows, macOS, Linux) that manages hardware and runs your apps.
CPU does general tasks fast in sequence; GPU does many simple tasks in parallel (graphics, AI).
A web address: protocol + domain + path, e.g. https://site.com/page.
A small piece of data a site stores in your browser, e.g. to keep you logged in.
Bandwidth is how much data per second; latency is the delay before it starts arriving.
